title: Navigation
description: Jetpack Navigation Compose, routes, and arguments
lesson:
Navigation in Compose

Jetpack Navigation Compose manages the back stack and screen transitions declaratively. Key concepts:

  • NavController — Manages the navigation back stack
  • NavHost — Container that swaps composable destinations
  • Route — String identifier for each destination (e.g., "detail/{id}")
NavHost(navController, startDestination = "home") { composable("home") { HomeScreen() } composable("detail/{id}") { DetailScreen() } }

Best practices: Keep routes as constants, use type-safe navigation (Kotlin serialization routes in newer versions), and handle the bottom navigation pattern with saveState/restoreState.

questions.title: NavHost Setup
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to set up navigation between Home and Detail screens.

blanks.hint: Method to navigate to a route

questions.explanation: rememberNavController() creates a NavController surviving recomposition. NavHost swaps destinations. composable("route") defines destinations. navigate("route") pushes onto the back stack.

questions.title: Navigation Argument Crash
questions.description: This crashes when navigating to detail: "argument is not a valid integer." Fix the route definition to properly handle integer arguments.

checks.message: Define the argument type with navArgument and NavType.IntType

questions.explanation: Navigation arguments default to String type. For integers, explicitly declare navArgument("itemId") { type = NavType.IntType } and use getInt().
hints: Navigation arguments default to String type
You need to explicitly declare the argument type

questions.title: Back Stack Behavior
questions.description: What does navController.navigate("settings") { popUpTo("home") { inclusive = true } } do?
options: Navigates to settings and clears the entire back stack
Pops everything up to and including home, then navigates to settings
Navigates to settings, keeping home in the back stack
Replaces settings entry if it exists
questions.explanation: popUpTo("home") { inclusive = true } pops all destinations up to and including "home", then navigates to "settings". This effectively resets the back stack. Common for login → main screen transitions.
hints: inclusive = true means the target is ALSO removed

questions.title: Bottom Navigation Pattern
questions.description: What navigation behavior does this code implement?
options: Simple tab switching — each click pushes a new destination
Tab switching with state management: saves/restores per-tab state, avoids duplicates
Replaces current destination, discarding all state
Opens each tab in a new window
questions.explanation: Three flags work together: saveState saves the state of tabs being left. launchSingleTop prevents duplicate instances. restoreState restores previous state when returning. This creates a smooth tab experience where each tab remembers its position.
hints: Three key flags: saveState, launchSingleTop, restoreState

questions.title: Deep Links
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to configure a composable destination that can be opened from a deep link URL.

blanks.hint: Builder function for deep link patterns

questions.explanation: deepLinks parameter accepts a list of navDeepLink patterns. When the system opens the URL "https://myapp.com/profile/123", Navigation automatically extracts "123" as the userId argument and navigates to this destination. Also requires intent-filter in AndroidManifest.xml.
hints: The parameter name matches the concept: links from external URLs
The builder function creates a deep link pattern with URL matching

questions.title: Type-Safe Navigation
questions.description: What is the main advantage of using Kotlin Serialization-based routes (introduced in Navigation 2.8+) over string-based routes?
options: Compile-time safety — route arguments are type-checked, eliminating runtime crashes from typos or wrong types
questions.explanation: String-based routes like "detail/{id}" are error-prone — typos, wrong types, and missing arguments are only caught at runtime. With Kotlin Serialization routes, you define routes as @Serializable data class Detail(val id: Int) and the compiler catches errors. This eliminates an entire class of navigation bugs.
hints: What goes wrong when you type "detial/{id}" instead of "detail/{id}"?
